Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}}

{{Short description|System of ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine rank") is a system of ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on rank. Higher ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}})
A shrine granted the highest rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[rank|rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the ranks granted to human officials. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}}. After the 11th century, martial ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位}} : Court ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族}}. Few examples exist of these ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine ranks|ja|社格}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human ranks, deity ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine ranks ultimately overshadowed deity ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, deity ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year }} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典}}. : As with human ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等}} : Martial ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}}. After the 11th century, martial ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位}} : Court ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族}}. Few examples exist of these ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine ranks|ja|社格}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human ranks, deity ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine ranks ultimately overshadowed deity ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, deity ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year }} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:original templates]][[ja:神階]] ==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]][[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:original templates]][[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]][[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:original templates]][[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|System of ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine rank") is a system of ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on rank. Higher ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}})
A shrine granted the highest rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[rank|rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary ranks (文位), martial ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary ranks ranged from the lowest rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank}}, spanning 30 ranks. For deities, the ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank}}, covering 15 ranks. The earliest record of a rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神}}—were granted ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典}}. : As with human ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等}} : Martial ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}}. After the 11th century, martial ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位}} : Court ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族}}. Few examples exist of these ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine ranks|ja|社格}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human ranks, deity ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine ranks ultimately overshadowed deity ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, deity ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year }} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:original templates]][[ja:神階]] ==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:original templates]][[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:original templates]][[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]][[Category:completion tier 4]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} [[Category:New]]

The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year }} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:original templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}

revision.comment: Bot: Replaced ill templates with appended |12=draft and created corresponding redirect pages.
revision.text:
{{Short description|System of ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine rank") is a system of ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)|12=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on rank. Higher ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|12=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|12=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[rank|rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary ranks (文位), martial ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|12=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary ranks ranged from the lowest rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, spanning 30 ranks. For deities, the ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, covering 15 ranks. The earliest record of a rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=draft}}—were granted ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}. : As with human ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}} : Martial ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|12=draft}} : Court ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=draft}}. Few examples exist of these ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|12=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine ranks|ja|社格|12=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human ranks, deity ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine ranks ultimately overshadowed deity ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, deity ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|12=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:original templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}

{{Short description|System of ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine rank") is a system of ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)|12=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on rank. Higher ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|12=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|12=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[rank|rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary ranks (文位), martial ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|12=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary ranks ranged from the lowest rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, spanning 30 ranks. For deities, the ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, covering 15 ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}. : As with human ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}} : Martial ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|12=draft}} : Court ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=draft}}. Few examples exist of these ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|12=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine ranks|ja|社格|12=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human ranks, deity ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine ranks ultimately overshadowed deity ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, deity ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|12=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:original templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}

Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)|12=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on rank. Higher ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|12=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|12=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[rank|rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary ranks (文位), martial ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|12=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary ranks ranged from the lowest rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, spanning 30 ranks. For deities, the ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, covering 15 ranks. The earliest record of a rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=draft}}—were granted ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}. : As with human ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}} : Martial ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|12=draft}} : Court ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=draft}}. Few examples exist of these ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|12=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine ranks|ja|社格|12=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human ranks, deity ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine ranks ultimately overshadowed deity ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, deity ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|12=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:original templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|System of ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine rank") is a system of ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)|12=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on rank. Higher ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|12=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|12=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[rank|rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary ranks (文位), martial ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|12=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary ranks ranged from the lowest rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, spanning 30 ranks. For deities, the ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, covering 15 ranks. The earliest record of a rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=draft}}—were granted ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}. : As with human ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}} : Martial ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|12=draft}} : Court ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=draft}}. Few examples exist of these ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|12=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine ranks|ja|社格|12=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human ranks, deity ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine ranks ultimately overshadowed deity ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, deity ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|12=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}

The system was identical to the rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary ranks (文位), martial ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|12=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary ranks ranged from the lowest rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, spanning 30 ranks. For deities, the ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft}}, covering 15 ranks. The earliest record of a rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=draft}}—were granted ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}. : As with human ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}} : Martial ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|12=draft}} : Court ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=draft}}. Few examples exist of these ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|12=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine ranks|ja|社格|12=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human ranks, deity ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine ranks ultimately overshadowed deity ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, deity ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|12=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}

{{Short description|System of ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine rank") is a system of ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)|12=draft|12=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on rank. Higher ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|12=draft|12=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|12=draft|12=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[rank|rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary ranks (文位), martial ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|12=draft|12=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary ranks ranged from the lowest rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft|12=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft|12=draft}}, spanning 30 ranks. For deities, the ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft|12=draft}}, covering 15 ranks. The earliest record of a rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=draft|12=draft}}—were granted ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft|12=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=draft|12=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=draft|12=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}. : As with human ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft|12=draft}} : Martial ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft|12=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=draft|12=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft|12=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft|12=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|12=draft|12=draft}} : Court ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=draft|12=draft}}. Few examples exist of these ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine ranks|ja|社格|12=draft|12=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human ranks, deity ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine ranks ultimately overshadowed deity ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft|12=draft}}, deity ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}

{{Short description|System of Ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the Rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine Rank") is a system of Ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher Rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and Ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system Ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system Ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]].
