Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and Rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine Rank)|lt=Rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine Rank)|12=draft|12=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on Rank. Higher Ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine Ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|12=draft|12=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|12=draft|12=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[Rank|Rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|12=draft|12=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft|12=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft|12=draft}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft|12=draft}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=draft|12=draft}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft|12=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=draft|12=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=draft|12=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft|12=draft}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft|12=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=draft|12=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft|12=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft|12=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|12=draft|12=draft}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=draft|12=draft}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|12=draft|12=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft|12=draft}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine Rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine Rank)}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinkai (divine Rank)]] [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine Rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine Rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine Rank)}}

{{Short description|System of Ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the Rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine Rank") is a system of Ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher Rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and Ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system Ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system Ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third Rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and Rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=Rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)|12=draft|12=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on Rank. Higher Ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine Ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|12=draft|12=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|12=draft|12=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[Rank|Rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|12=draft|12=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft|12=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft|12=draft}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=draft|12=draft}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=draft|12=draft}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft|12=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=draft|12=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=draft|12=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft|12=draft}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=draft|12=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=draft|12=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft|12=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft|12=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|12=draft|12=draft}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=draft|12=draft}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|12=draft|12=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft|12=draft}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}}

The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}} [[Category:Ambox transclusions]]

{{Short description|System of Ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the Rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine Rank") is a system of Ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher Rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and Ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system Ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system Ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third Rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and Rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=Rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on Rank. Higher Ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine Ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|qq=draft|qq=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|qq=draft|qq=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[Rank|Rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}} [[Category:Ambox transclusions]]

revision.comment: 67 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:神階 up to rev 103856860
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|[[賀茂別雷神社]]（[[京都府]][[京都市]]）
極位である「正一位勲一等」を授けられた一社。]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|[[宇佐神宮]]（[[大分県]][[宇佐市]]）
珍しい「一品」の品位が授けられた一社。]] '''神階'''（しんかい）または'''神位'''（しんい）は、[[日本]]において[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]に授けられた[[位階]]。 == 概要 == 神階とは、人臣に授けられた位階を神にも授けたものである。より正確には、位階は人にも神にも区別なく授けられたもので、そのうち神に授けられたものが神階と呼ばれている。仕組みは人臣に対する位階と同じで、文位（狭義の位階）・武位（[[勲等|勲位]]・[[勲等]]）・[[品位 (位階)|品位]]の3種がある。 ; 文位・位階 : 文位（ぶんい）・位階（いかい）として、人に対しては[[少初位|少初位下]]から[[正一位]]までの30階があるが、神に対するものは[[正六位]]から正一位までの15階がある。神に位階を授けた最初の記録は『[[日本書紀]]』において[[天武天皇]]元年（[[673年]]）7月に[[壬申の乱]]に際して霊験を現した[[大和国]]の[[河俣神社|高市御県坐鴨事代主神]]（たけちのみあがにますかものことしろぬしのかみ）、[[牟佐坐神社|牟狭坐神]]（むさにますのかみ）、[[村屋坐弥冨都比売神社|村屋坐弥富都比売神]]（むらやにますみふつひめのかみ）に位を授与したとする記述であるただし、ここに記されている「品」は等級程度の意味で、『[[新抄格勅符抄]]』にある「気比神に従三位料として200戸を与えた」とする[[天平]]3年（[[731年]]）12月10日の記事が神階授与の最初とされることもある（「八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開」（小倉慈司））。。[[嘉祥]]4年（[[851年]]）には全国の[[神社]]の祭神に正六位以上の神階が贈られ、次第に濫授されるようになった『国史大辞典』神階項。。 : 人の位階同様、「正○位」の「正」は「しょう」、「従○位」の「従」は「じゅ」と読む。また「三位」は「さんみ」、「四位」は「しい」と読む。 ; 武位・勲位・勲等 : 武位（ぶい）・勲位（くんい）・勲等（くんとう）として、人・神とも勲十二等から勲一等までの12等がある。人に対しては武勲を上げた者に与えられたが、[[7世紀]]半ばからそれ以外の者にも与えられるようになった。神に対しても同様である。神に勲位を与えた記述の初見は[[天平神護]]元年（[[765年]]）、[[藤原仲麻呂の乱|恵美押勝の乱]]で霊験を現した[[近江国]]の[[都久夫須麻神社|都久夫須麻神]]（つくぶすまのかみ）に勲八等を与えたとするものである。[[11世紀]]以降は神に対する勲位の授与は行われていない。 ; 品位 : 品位（ほんい）として、人については[[皇族]]に授けられるものである。神に対しては授けられた例はあまりない。[[天平勝宝]]元年（[[749年]]）12月、豊前国の[[宇佐八幡]]の大神（[[八幡神]]）に対して一品（いっぽん）を、その[[比売神]]に対して二品（にほん）を与えた例などがある。 [[神社]]ごとに定められる[[社格]]に対し、神階は神に与えられるものである。人に対する位階はそれによって就ける官職が異なったり[[位田]]が支給されたりなどの特典を伴うものであったのと同じように、神に対する位階は初期には食封・位田を伴っていた「八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開」（小倉慈司）。。しかしのちにその給与はなくなって単なる栄誉的性格を強め、神階よりも神社の社格の方が重視されていた。また、[[分祀]]の際には神階は引き継がれず神階を引き継ぐ場合には勅許が必要であった。しかし、[[律令制]]の崩壊とともに分祀先でも勧請元の神社の神階を名乗る神社も現れた。特に[[稲荷神社]]は総本社である[[伏見稲荷大社]]が正一位であるとして、そこから勧請を受けた稲荷神社も正一位を称している。 神階の授与は[[神祇官]]や諸国からの申請に基づいて[[公卿]]の会議で議論され、[[天皇]]への奏聞を経て決定された。しかし、[[平安時代]]になると[[神祇官]]や[[国司]]が勝手に神階を授与するということもたびたび行われるようになった。[[中世]]以降は[[吉田家]]も「'''宗源宣旨'''（そうげんせんじ）」といわれる[[宣旨]]で神階を発行するようになった。宗源宣旨は当初は天皇の[[勅|勅許]]の下に発行されていたが、のちに吉田家が独自に発行するようになった。 [[江戸時代]]の[[寛保]]3年[[12月15日 (旧暦)|12月15日]]（[[1744年]][[1月29日]]）、[[桜町天皇]]と関白[[一条兼香]]は神階の授与に関する最終決定は勅許によるものに限定し、吉田家による宗源宣旨の発行は停止されて同家は天皇への執奏のみを行うこととされた。[[京都所司代]][[牧野貞通]]や[[寺社奉行]][[大岡忠相]]もこれを承知していることから、決定については[[江戸幕府]]の同意を得ていたと考えられている。また、江戸幕府も神階授与に関与していた形跡があるが、あくまでも幕府が主体となって神階授与を働きかけた際に主導的な役割を果たしたものであると考えられ、幕府との関係性が薄い地方の神社の神階授与までは関与しなかったとみられる。前者の場合には幕府からの[[下行米]]が朝廷に贈られることがあったが、後者は原則として神社側の費用負担が前提になっていた（これに対して、[[神号]]授与に関しては江戸幕府の同意が必要であった）。江戸時代を通じて神階授与が65件しか確認されない（うち27件が[[1850年代]]以降に集中している）のは、神社側の負担の大きさも影響しているとみられている間瀬久美子「神社と天皇」（初出:高埜利彦・永原慶二 他編『講座 前近代の天皇 3 天皇と社会諸集団』青木書店、1993年/所収:間瀬『近世朝廷の権威と寺社・民衆』吉川弘文館、2022年）2022年、P127-135・152.。 神階の制度は[[明治|明治時代]]に廃止されたが、現在も社名に神階をつけている神社がある。 == 変遷 == === 六国史終了時点での神階一覧 === [[六国史]]終了時点（[[887年]]）での各神の神階の一覧[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/jinjaindex.html 神社史料集成]（國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム）を参考に掲載。。
文位（位階） 一覧
六国史終了時点の従三位以上の位階の一覧。なお、六国史以後の濫授の結果、現在では非常に多くの神社が正一位に達している。 神名は神階授与時の表記で、括弧内は『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]での鎮座国と現神社名（複数のものは[[論社]]）。 '''なし（位階を超越）''' * [[天照大御神|伊勢大神]] （[[伊勢国]] [[伊勢神宮|神宮]]） * [[日前大神|日前神]] （[[紀伊国]] [[日前神宮・國懸神宮|日前神宮]]） * [[國懸大神|国懸神]] （紀伊国 [[日前神宮・國懸神宮|國懸神宮]]） '''[[正一位]]''' * [[神産巣日神|神産日神]]、[[高御産巣日神|高御産日神]]、[[玉積産日神]]、[[足産日神]] （宮中 [[八神殿]]のち[[宮中三殿#神殿|神殿]]） * 松尾神 （[[山城国]] [[松尾大社]]） * 今木神 （山城国 [[平野神社]]） * [[賀茂別雷神]] （山城国 [[賀茂別雷神社]]） * [[賀茂建角身命|賀茂御祖神]] （山城国 [[賀茂御祖神社]]） * [[春日神]] （[[大和国]] [[春日大社]]） * [[大己貴神]] （大和国 [[大名持神社]]） * [[大物主神|大神大物主神]] （大和国 [[大神神社]]） * [[布都御魂|石上神]] （大和国 [[石上神宮]]） * [[天児屋命|枚岡天児屋根命]] （[[河内国]] [[枚岡神社]]） * [[経津主神|伊波比主命神]] （[[下総国]] [[香取神宮]]） * [[建御雷神|建御賀豆智命神]] （[[常陸国]] [[鹿島神宮]]） * [[大山咋神|大比叡神]] （近江国 [[日吉大社]]） '''[[従一位]]''' * [[生産日神]] （宮中 [[八神殿]]のち[[宮中三殿#神殿|神殿]]） * [[田心姫神]]、[[湍津姫神]]、[[市杵島姫神]] （京中<式外社> 不詳） * [[御歳神|葛木御歳神]] （大和国 [[葛木御歳神社]]） * [[アヂスキタカヒコネ|高鴨阿治須岐宅比古尼神]] （大和国 [[高鴨神社]]） * 宗像神 （大和国 [[宗像神社 (桜井市)|宗像神社]]） * [[事代主神|高市御県鴨八重事代主神]] （大和国 [[河俣神社]]） * [[高良大社]]） '''[[正二位]]''' * [[月読命|葛野月読神]] （山城国 [[月読神社 (京都市)|月読神社]]） * [[天津日子根命|多度神]] （伊勢国 [[多度大社]]） * 熱田神 （[[尾張国]] [[熱田神宮]]） * [[金山彦|中山金山彦神]] （[[美濃国]] [[南宮大社]]） * [[八坂刀売神|建御名方富命前八坂刀自命神]] （信濃国 [[諏訪大社]]） * 二荒神 （[[下野国]] [[宇都宮二荒山神社]]、[[日光二荒山神社]]） * [[火遠理命|若狭比古神]] （[[若狭国]] [[若狭彦神社]]） * [[須佐之男命|熊野神]] （[[出雲国]] [[熊野大社]]） * 杵築神 （出雲国 [[出雲大社]]） * [[熊野権現|熊野早玉神]] （紀伊国 [[熊野速玉大社]]） * [[大山積神]] （[[伊予国]] [[大山祇神社]]） * 市杵島姫神、湍津姫神、田心姫神 （[[筑前国]] [[宗像大社]]） * [[健磐龍命|健磐竜命神]] （[[肥後国]] [[阿蘇神社]]） '''[[従二位]]''' * [[一言主|葛木一言主神]] （大和国 [[葛城一言主神社]]） * 高天彦神 （大和国 [[高天彦神社]]） * [[火雷神|葛木火雷神]] （大和国 [[葛木坐火雷神社]]） * 薦枕高御産栖日神 （大和国<式外社> 宇奈太理坐高御魂神社か） * [[御食津臣命|恩智大御食津比古命神]]、恩智大御食津比咩命神 （河内国 [[恩智神社]]） * 弥加布都命神 （河内国<式外社> 不詳） * 比古佐自布都命神 （河内国<式外社> 不詳） * [[武美名別命|武水別神]] （信濃国 [[武水別神社]]） * [[大物忌神]] （[[出羽国]] [[鳥海山大物忌神社]]） * 月山神 （出羽国 [[月山神社]]） * [[豊玉毘売命|若狭比咩神]] （若狭国 [[若狭彦神社|若狭姫神社]]） * 熊野坐神 （紀伊国 [[熊野本宮大社]]） '''[[正三位]]''' * [[園神]] （宮中 [[園韓神社|園神社]]<廃社>） * [[韓神]] （宮中 [[園韓神社|韓神社]]<廃社>） * [[久度神]]、[[古開神]] （山城国 [[平野神社]]） * 春日神 （大和国 [[龍田大社]]） * 広瀬神 品位 一覧
六国史終了時点の品位の一覧。神名は神階授与時の表記で、括弧内は『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]での鎮座国と現神社名。 '''[[一品]]''' * 伊佐奈岐命 （[[淡路国]] [[伊弉諾神宮]]） * 八幡神、八幡比咩神 （[[豊前国]] [[宇佐神宮]]） '''[[二品]]''' * [[吉備都彦命]] （[[備中国]] [[吉備津神社]]）
=== 諸神同時昇叙 === 平安時代中期以降には、諸神に対する同時昇叙の例があった。文献に見える昇叙例は次の通り[[宮地直一]] [{{NDLDC|943702/108}} 『神祇史綱要』]（明治書院、大正8年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）、108-109コマ参照。。 {| class="wikitable" style="margin:0 auto;text-align:left;background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%" !年月日!!範囲!!昇叙理由 |- |[[嘉祥]]3年（[[850年]]）12月8日||天下大小諸神||即位の際の恩典 |- |[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]元年（[[859年]]）1月27日||267社||即位の際の恩典 |- |[[寛平]]9年（[[897年]]）12月13日||340社||即位の際の恩典 |- |[[天慶]]3年（[[940年]]）1月6日||天下又は諸国の諸神||兵革の祈祷（[[承平天慶の乱#平将門の乱|平将門の乱]]鎮撫）
極位（正一位）の際は封戸を奉献 |- |[[永保]]元年（[[1081年]]）2月10日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[永治]]元年（[[1141年]]）7月10日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[治承]]元年（[[1177年]]）12月12日||天下又は諸国の諸神||兵革の祈祷（[[鹿ケ谷の陰謀]]） |- |[[元暦]]2年（[[1185年]]）3月4日||天下又は諸国の諸神||兵革の祈祷（平家滅亡） |- |[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）2月13日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月20日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[建治]]元年（[[1275年]]）7月20日||天下又は諸国の諸神||兵革の祈祷（[[元寇]]準備開始）
極位の際は禰宜・祝らに叙位 |- |[[元亨]]元年（[[1321年]]）3月28日||諸神||辛酉革命
一階を進める例文は見えず |- |[[永徳]]元年（[[1381年]]）2月24日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[文亀]]元年（[[1501年]]）2月29日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |} 諸神同時昇叙において、嘉祥4年（851年）には既に従五位下以上の諸神には一階を増し、大社並の名神は無位でも従五位下を授け、これ以外の無位の諸神を正六位上に叙した。こうした諸神増一階はその後、天慶3年から永徳元年（平安中期から室町初期）までに9回行われたという嘉祥3年（850年）から寛平9年（897年）までに正五位上以上に昇叙された諸神は、遅くとも文亀元年（1501年）に正一位に昇叙となる。少なくない二階昇叙があった例を考慮せず、貞観元年、寛平9年での指定外、元亨元年の未実施を考慮・除外した場合でもこれに含まれる。。室町時代中頃以降の初叙には年代通算相当の位階を奉ったが、その場合には貞観・寛平・元亨年度の加算を除くことが例とされた。 なお神階で諸神序列が進んだ時代には、貞観元年（859年）『日本三代実録』貞観元年7月13日・14日条。の清和天皇の時を初例とし仁和2年（888年）に制度化された「一代一度大神宝使の制」『日本紀略』仁和4年（888年）11月条、昌泰元年（898年）7月条・8月条、承平2年（932年）9月条など（宇多天皇・醍醐天皇・朱雀天皇の御世）。があった。これは、天皇の一代一度に、即位大典ごとに伊勢大神宮並びに五畿七道各国の諸名神に神宝・御幣を捧げて即位を告げたものである。朝廷から奉献対象は66カ国のうちから京畿七道の50社で、鎌倉時代中期まで継続したという。「一代一度大神宝使」発遣の儀礼内容は、『左経記』寛仁元年（1017年）10月2日条などに詳述されている。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]』（[[吉川弘文館]]）神階項 {{神道 横}} {{日本の位階}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんかい}} [[Category:位階制度]] [[Category:神道]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[神階]] to [[Shinkai (divine rank)]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|[[賀茂別雷神社]]（[[京都府]][[京都市]]）
極位である「正一位勲一等」を授けられた一社。]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|[[宇佐神宮]]（[[大分県]][[宇佐市]]）
珍しい「一品」の品位が授けられた一社。]] '''神階'''（しんかい）または'''神位'''（しんい）は、[[日本]]において[[神道]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]に授けられた[[位階]]。 == 概要 == 神階とは、人臣に授けられた位階を神にも授けたものである。より正確には、位階は人にも神にも区別なく授けられたもので、そのうち神に授けられたものが神階と呼ばれている。仕組みは人臣に対する位階と同じで、文位（狭義の位階）・武位（[[勲等|勲位]]・[[勲等]]）・[[品位 (位階)|品位]]の3種がある。 ; 文位・位階 : 文位（ぶんい）・位階（いかい）として、人に対しては[[少初位|少初位下]]から[[正一位]]までの30階があるが、神に対するものは[[正六位]]から正一位までの15階がある。神に位階を授けた最初の記録は『[[日本書紀]]』において[[天武天皇]]元年（[[673年]]）7月に[[壬申の乱]]に際して霊験を現した[[大和国]]の[[河俣神社|高市御県坐鴨事代主神]]（たけちのみあがにますかものことしろぬしのかみ）、[[牟佐坐神社|牟狭坐神]]（むさにますのかみ）、[[村屋坐弥冨都比売神社|村屋坐弥富都比売神]]（むらやにますみふつひめのかみ）に位を授与したとする記述であるただし、ここに記されている「品」は等級程度の意味で、『[[新抄格勅符抄]]』にある「気比神に従三位料として200戸を与えた」とする[[天平]]3年（[[731年]]）12月10日の記事が神階授与の最初とされることもある（「八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開」（小倉慈司））。。[[嘉祥]]4年（[[851年]]）には全国の[[神社]]の祭神に正六位以上の神階が贈られ、次第に濫授されるようになった『国史大辞典』神階項。。 : 人の位階同様、「正○位」の「正」は「しょう」、「従○位」の「従」は「じゅ」と読む。また「三位」は「さんみ」、「四位」は「しい」と読む。 ; 武位・勲位・勲等 : 武位（ぶい）・勲位（くんい）・勲等（くんとう）として、人・神とも勲十二等から勲一等までの12等がある。人に対しては武勲を上げた者に与えられたが、[[7世紀]]半ばからそれ以外の者にも与えられるようになった。神に対しても同様である。神に勲位を与えた記述の初見は[[天平神護]]元年（[[765年]]）、[[藤原仲麻呂の乱|恵美押勝の乱]]で霊験を現した[[近江国]]の[[都久夫須麻神社|都久夫須麻神]]（つくぶすまのかみ）に勲八等を与えたとするものである。[[11世紀]]以降は神に対する勲位の授与は行われていない。 ; 品位 : 品位（ほんい）として、人については[[皇族]]に授けられるものである。神に対しては授けられた例はあまりない。[[天平勝宝]]元年（[[749年]]）12月、豊前国の[[宇佐八幡]]の大神（[[八幡神]]）に対して一品（いっぽん）を、その[[比売神]]に対して二品（にほん）を与えた例などがある。 [[神社]]ごとに定められる[[社格]]に対し、神階は神に与えられるものである。人に対する位階はそれによって就ける官職が異なったり[[位田]]が支給されたりなどの特典を伴うものであったのと同じように、神に対する位階は初期には食封・位田を伴っていた「八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開」（小倉慈司）。。しかしのちにその給与はなくなって単なる栄誉的性格を強め、神階よりも神社の社格の方が重視されていた。また、[[分祀]]の際には神階は引き継がれず神階を引き継ぐ場合には勅許が必要であった。しかし、[[律令制]]の崩壊とともに分祀先でも勧請元の神社の神階を名乗る神社も現れた。特に[[稲荷神社]]は総本社である[[伏見稲荷大社]]が正一位であるとして、そこから勧請を受けた稲荷神社も正一位を称している。 神階の授与は[[神祇官]]や諸国からの申請に基づいて[[公卿]]の会議で議論され、[[天皇]]への奏聞を経て決定された。しかし、[[平安時代]]になると[[神祇官]]や[[国司]]が勝手に神階を授与するということもたびたび行われるようになった。[[中世]]以降は[[吉田家]]も「'''宗源宣旨'''（そうげんせんじ）」といわれる[[宣旨]]で神階を発行するようになった。宗源宣旨は当初は天皇の[[勅|勅許]]の下に発行されていたが、のちに吉田家が独自に発行するようになった。 [[江戸時代]]の[[寛保]]3年[[12月15日 (旧暦)|12月15日]]（[[1744年]][[1月29日]]）、[[桜町天皇]]と関白[[一条兼香]]は神階の授与に関する最終決定は勅許によるものに限定し、吉田家による宗源宣旨の発行は停止されて同家は天皇への執奏のみを行うこととされた。[[京都所司代]][[牧野貞通]]や[[寺社奉行]][[大岡忠相]]もこれを承知していることから、決定については[[江戸幕府]]の同意を得ていたと考えられている。また、江戸幕府も神階授与に関与していた形跡があるが、あくまでも幕府が主体となって神階授与を働きかけた際に主導的な役割を果たしたものであると考えられ、幕府との関係性が薄い地方の神社の神階授与までは関与しなかったとみられる。前者の場合には幕府からの[[下行米]]が朝廷に贈られることがあったが、後者は原則として神社側の費用負担が前提になっていた（これに対して、[[神号]]授与に関しては江戸幕府の同意が必要であった）。江戸時代を通じて神階授与が65件しか確認されない（うち27件が[[1850年代]]以降に集中している）のは、神社側の負担の大きさも影響しているとみられている間瀬久美子「神社と天皇」（初出:高埜利彦・永原慶二 他編『講座 前近代の天皇 3 天皇と社会諸集団』青木書店、1993年/所収:間瀬『近世朝廷の権威と寺社・民衆』吉川弘文館、2022年）2022年、P127-135・152.。 神階の制度は[[明治|明治時代]]に廃止されたが、現在も社名に神階をつけている神社がある。 == 変遷 == === 六国史終了時点での神階一覧 === [[六国史]]終了時点（[[887年]]）での各神の神階の一覧[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/jinjaindex.html 神社史料集成]（國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム）を参考に掲載。。

品位 一覧
六国史終了時点の品位の一覧。神名は神階授与時の表記で、括弧内は『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]での鎮座国と現神社名。 '''[[一品]]''' * 伊佐奈岐命 （[[淡路国]] [[伊弉諾神宮]]） * 八幡神、八幡比咩神 （[[豊前国]] [[宇佐神宮]]） '''[[二品]]''' * [[吉備都彦命]] （[[備中国]] [[吉備津神社]]）
=== 諸神同時昇叙 === 平安時代中期以降には、諸神に対する同時昇叙の例があった。文献に見える昇叙例は次の通り[[宮地直一]] [{{NDLDC|943702/108}} 『神祇史綱要』]（明治書院、大正8年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）、108-109コマ参照。。 {| class="wikitable" style="margin:0 auto;text-align:left;background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%" !年月日!!範囲!!昇叙理由 |- |[[嘉祥]]3年（[[850年]]）12月8日||天下大小諸神||即位の際の恩典 |- |[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]元年（[[859年]]）1月27日||267社||即位の際の恩典 |- |[[寛平]]9年（[[897年]]）12月13日||340社||即位の際の恩典 |- |[[天慶]]3年（[[940年]]）1月6日||天下又は諸国の諸神||兵革の祈祷（[[承平天慶の乱#平将門の乱|平将門の乱]]鎮撫）
極位（正一位）の際は封戸を奉献 |- |[[永保]]元年（[[1081年]]）2月10日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[永治]]元年（[[1141年]]）7月10日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[治承]]元年（[[1177年]]）12月12日||天下又は諸国の諸神||兵革の祈祷（[[鹿ケ谷の陰謀]]） |- |[[元暦]]2年（[[1185年]]）3月4日||天下又は諸国の諸神||兵革の祈祷（平家滅亡） |- |[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）2月13日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月20日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[建治]]元年（[[1275年]]）7月20日||天下又は諸国の諸神||兵革の祈祷（[[元寇]]準備開始）
極位の際は禰宜・祝らに叙位 |- |[[元亨]]元年（[[1321年]]）3月28日||諸神||辛酉革命
一階を進める例文は見えず |- |[[永徳]]元年（[[1381年]]）2月24日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |- |[[文亀]]元年（[[1501年]]）2月29日||天下又は諸国の諸神||辛酉革命 |} 諸神同時昇叙において、嘉祥4年（851年）には既に従五位下以上の諸神には一階を増し、大社並の名神は無位でも従五位下を授け、これ以外の無位の諸神を正六位上に叙した。こうした諸神増一階はその後、天慶3年から永徳元年（平安中期から室町初期）までに9回行われたという嘉祥3年（850年）から寛平9年（897年）までに正五位上以上に昇叙された諸神は、遅くとも文亀元年（1501年）に正一位に昇叙となる。少なくない二階昇叙があった例を考慮せず、貞観元年、寛平9年での指定外、元亨元年の未実施を考慮・除外した場合でもこれに含まれる。。室町時代中頃以降の初叙には年代通算相当の位階を奉ったが、その場合には貞観・寛平・元亨年度の加算を除くことが例とされた。 なお神階で諸神序列が進んだ時代には、貞観元年（859年）『日本三代実録』貞観元年7月13日・14日条。の清和天皇の時を初例とし仁和2年（888年）に制度化された「一代一度大神宝使の制」『日本紀略』仁和4年（888年）11月条、昌泰元年（898年）7月条・8月条、承平2年（932年）9月条など（宇多天皇・醍醐天皇・朱雀天皇の御世）。があった。これは、天皇の一代一度に、即位大典ごとに伊勢大神宮並びに五畿七道各国の諸名神に神宝・御幣を捧げて即位を告げたものである。朝廷から奉献対象は66カ国のうちから京畿七道の50社で、鎌倉時代中期まで継続したという。「一代一度大神宝使」発遣の儀礼内容は、『左経記』寛仁元年（1017年）10月2日条などに詳述されている。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]』（[[吉川弘文館]]）神階項 {{神道 横}} {{日本の位階}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんかい}} [[Category:位階制度]] [[Category:神道]]

{{Short description|System of Ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the Rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine Rank") is a system of Ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher Rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and Ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system Ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system Ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third Rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and Rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=Rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on Rank. Higher Ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine Ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|qq=draft|qq=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|qq=draft|qq=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[Rank|Rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}} [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] {{translated page|ja|神階|version=103856860|comment=Imported full ja history}}

A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[Rank|Rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{translated page|ja|神階|version=103856860|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]]

{{Short description|System of Ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever [[Mount Chokai]] (right) erupted, the Rank of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine Rank") is a system of Ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher Rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the [[Myōjin]] and Ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system Ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Kokuhei-shōsha|kokuhei-sha]] system Ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third Rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the [[Enryaku|Enryaku era]], the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and Rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time [[Mount Chōkai|Mount Chokai]] erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=Rank|ja|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of [[Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of [[Emperor Yōmei]].{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on Rank. Higher Ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine Ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|qq=draft|qq=draft}})
A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingu|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Usa City|ja|宇佐市|qq=draft|qq=draft}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the [[Rank|Rank system]] of [[Japan]], whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位 (位階)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Lesser Initial Rank|lt=Lesser Initial Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks|ja|品位|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman God|ja|八幡神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{citation needed|Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{translated page|ja|神階|version=103856860|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Original templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]]
