revision.text:
[[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[fa:شینکای (رتبه الهی)]][[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[fa:شینکای (رتبه الهی)]][[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[fa:شینکای (رتبه الهی)]][[ja:神階]] {{Short description|System of Ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chōkai}} (right) erupted, the Rank of [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine Rank") is a system of Ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher Rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the {{ill|Myōjin|en|Myōjin|WD=Q5369590|lt=Myōjin}} and Ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system Ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|kokuhei-sha]] system Ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[:en:Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third Rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku era}}, the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and Rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chokai}} erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|en|Shinkai (divine rank)|WD=Q11591025|lt=Rank}} of [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei}}.{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on Rank. Higher Ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine Ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|en|Kamigamo Shrine|WD=Q700448|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City}})
A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingu}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture}}, {{ill|Usa, Ōita|en|Usa, Ōita|WD=Q114147|lt=Usa City}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the {{ill|Rank|en|Rank|WD=Q4120621|lt=Rank system}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位 (位階)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kehi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial family members|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman God}} of {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Shrine}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-no-Kami}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Rikkokushi|en|Rikkokushi|WD=Q1151168|lt=Six National Histories}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{translated page|ja|神階|version=103856860|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{wikidata link|Q11591025}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

For example In history every time {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chokai}} erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|en|Shinkai (divine rank)|WD=Q11591025|lt=Rank}} of [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei}}.{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on Rank. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|en|Kamigamo Shrine|WD=Q700448|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City}})
A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingu}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture}}, {{ill|Usa, Ōita|en|Usa, Ōita|WD=Q114147|lt=Usa City}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the {{ill|Rank|en|Rank|WD=Q4120621|lt=Rank system}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位 (位階)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kehi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial family members|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman God}} of {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Shrine}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-no-Kami}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Rikkokushi|en|Rikkokushi|WD=Q1151168|lt=Six National Histories}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{translated page|ja|神階|version=103856860|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{wikidata link|Q11591025}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

revision.text:
[[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[fa:شینکای (رتبه الهی)]][[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[fa:شینکای (رتبه الهی)]][[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[fa:شینکای (رتبه الهی)]][[ja:神階]] {{Short description|System of Ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chōkai|1=Mount Chōkai}} (right) erupted, the Rank of [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine Rank") is a system of Ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher Rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the {{ill|Myōjin|en|Myōjin|WD=Q5369590|lt=Myōjin|1=Myōjin}} and Ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system Ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|kokuhei-sha]] system Ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[:en:Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third Rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku era|1=Enryaku}}, the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and Rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chokai|1=Mount Chōkai}} erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|en|Shinkai (divine rank)|WD=Q11591025|lt=Rank|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}}.{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on Rank. Higher Ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine Ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|en|Kamigamo Shrine|WD=Q700448|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|1=Kamigamo Shrine}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}})
A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingu|1=Usa Jingū}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, {{ill|Usa, Ōita|en|Usa, Ōita|WD=Q114147|lt=Usa City|1=Usa, Ōita}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the {{ill|Rank|en|Rank|WD=Q4120621|lt=Rank system|1=Rank}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位 (位階)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kehi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial family members|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman God|1=Hachiman}} of {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Shrine|1=Usa Jingū}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-no-Kami|1=Himegami}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shakaku|WD=Q10444029}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Rikkokushi|en|Rikkokushi|WD=Q1151168|lt=Six National Histories|1=Rikkokushi}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{translated page|ja|神階|version=103856860|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{wikidata link|Q11591025}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[fa:شینکای (رتبه الهی)]][[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[fa:شینکای (رتبه الهی)]][[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]][[fa:شینکای (رتبه الهی)]][[ja:神階]] {{Short description|System of Ranking deities in Japan}} [[File:七高山から新山の眺め.jpg|thumb|Whenever {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chōkai|1=Mount Chōkai}} (right) erupted, the Rank of [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] the god of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] (left) was raised]] '''''Shinkai''''' (神階, "divine Rank") is a system of Ranking [[kami]] in [[Shinto]].{{Cite journal |last=慈司 |first=小倉 |date=1994 |title=八・九世紀における地方神社行政の展開 (English language abstract only) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/shigaku/103/3/103_KJ00003671724/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=史学雑誌 |volume=103 |issue=3 |pages=390–415, 476–47 |doi=10.24471/shigaku.103.3_390}}{{Cite web |title=SHIGAKU ZASSHI |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/browse/shigaku/103/3/_contents/-char/en |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=www.jstage.jst.go.jp |language=en}} Higher Rank meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == History == The Shinkai system was linked to the {{ill|Myōjin|en|Myōjin|WD=Q5369590|lt=Myōjin|1=Myōjin}} and Ranked deity shrine systems. The Myojin system Ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|kokuhei-sha]] system Ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. At first, the state shrine system did not work well. It was meant for festivals for [[:en:Japanese deities|Japan's deities]]. At that time only a few state shrines existed. Their numbers started to grow in the mid-eighth century. This was because of changes in local governance. These changes were part of the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. The first mention of ''shinkai'' is in the ''Tôdaiji yôroku''. This document is from around 1134. It says that in 746, [[Hachiman]] [[Taisha (shrine)|Taisha]] got the third Rank. This was to help an ill emperor{{who|date=December 2023}}. The earlier Hanpei system was about giving festival materials (''heihaku'') to shrines. The system got stricter. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. During the {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku era|1=Enryaku}}, the [[Department of Divinities]] made two important changes: * Specially Designated Shrines and Deities: This system managed some shrines (Kanpei-kokuhei-sha) and deities (Myojin). The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. They applied for recognition from the Bureau. They also asked for Heihaku. By the late eighth century, it was common to give status and Rankings to shrines. This created a clear hierarchy. Governors and local leaders supported this. Governors liked it for control. Local leaders liked it for political status. For example In history every time {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chokai|1=Mount Chōkai}} erupted, the {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|en|Shinkai (divine rank)|WD=Q11591025|lt=Rank|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] the kami of [[Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com}} was increased.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}}.{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en}} At first, shrines got land based on Rank. Higher Ranks meant more land. Governors gradually got more control over shrine Ranking. Ranking gods became mainly a provincial matter. The central government was little involved. Later, the practice changed. It became more ceremonial and less practical. The system ended in the Meiji period. ==overview== {{Cite_sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 07:45 (UTC)}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|en|Kamigamo Shrine|WD=Q700448|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|1=Kamigamo Shrine}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}})
A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingu|1=Usa Jingū}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, {{ill|Usa, Ōita|en|Usa, Ōita|WD=Q114147|lt=Usa City|1=Usa, Ōita}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the {{ill|Rank|en|Rank|WD=Q4120621|lt=Rank system|1=Rank}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位 (位階)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kehi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial family members|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman God|1=Hachiman}} of {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Shrine|1=Usa Jingū}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-no-Kami|1=Himegami}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shakaku|WD=Q10444029}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Rikkokushi|en|Rikkokushi|WD=Q1151168|lt=Six National Histories|1=Rikkokushi}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{translated page|ja|神階|version=103856860|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{wikidata link|Q11591025}} [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

The Myojin system Ranked deities and the Kanpei [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|kokuhei-sha]] system Ranked shrines. Its goal was to control important shrines for the state. Shinto priests who did not collect ''heihaku'' faced penalties, but this did not work well. The system changed a lot in the late eighth century under the [[Ritsuryō|Ritsuryo system]]. Many thought the [[Department of Divinities]] was losing power. But the number of state-supported shrines grew. The Bureau managed them directly in the Kinai region. Provincial governors (Kokushi) managed them in other regions. * Increased Control: Provincial governors tried to get more control. A shrine granted the highest Rank of "Senior First Rank and First Order of Merit."]] [[File:Usa Shrine Nanchu Romon.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingu|1=Usa Jingū}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, {{ill|Usa, Ōita|en|Usa, Ōita|WD=Q114147|lt=Usa City|1=Usa, Ōita}})
A rare example of a shrine granted the "First Class" of dignity.]] '''Shinkai''' (神階) or '''Shin'i''' (神位) refers to the {{ill|Rank|en|Rank|WD=Q4120621|lt=Rank system|1=Rank}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, whereby a Rank was granted to deities of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}}. == Overview == Shinkai refers to the Ranks conferred upon deities in a manner similar to the Ranks granted to human officials. More precisely, Ranks were granted indiscriminately to both humans and deities, with those granted to deities specifically referred to as Shinkai. The system was identical to the Rank system for human officials, categorized into three types: literary Ranks (文位), martial Ranks (武位 or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}), and {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位 (位階)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}}. ; Literary Rank (文位) / Court Rank (位階) : For humans, literary Ranks ranged from the lowest Rank of {{ill|Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}} to the highest {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, spanning 30 Ranks. For deities, the Ranks ranged from {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}}, covering 15 Ranks. The earliest record of a Rank being granted to a deity is found in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, which describes how, in the first year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (673), three deities—{{ill|Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|高市御県坐鴨事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takichi-no-Mikenasukamo-Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja|牟佐坐神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa-ni-Masu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja|村屋坐弥富都比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraya-ni-Masu-Mifutsu-Hime-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—were granted Ranks due to their efficacy in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}.However, the "grade" mentioned here might refer to their importance rather than Ranks. Some scholars argue that the first instance of Rank conferment to deities was in 731 during Emperor Shōmu's reign, as recorded in the {{ill|Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Kakyō Fukushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, where 200 households were granted to {{ill|Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Jingū|lt=Kehi Shrine|ja|気比神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kehi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for their services. By 851 (Kashō 4), all deities enshrined in {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} nationwide were granted at least Senior Sixth Rank, marking the start of widespread conferment.See "Shinkai" in the {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}. : As with human Ranks, "正" (Senior) is pronounced "shō," and "従" (Junior) is pronounced "ju." "三位" is read as "sanmi," and "四位" as "shii." ; Martial Rank (武位) / {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} : Martial Ranks, or {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|勲等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of Merit|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, included 12 levels, from {{ill|Twelfth Order of Merit|ja|勲十二等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}} to {{ill|First Order of Merit|ja|勲一等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Order of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}. Initially granted to humans for military achievements, they were later extended to deities. The first recorded instance of martial Ranks for deities was in 765 (Tenpyō Jingo 1), when {{ill|Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja|都久夫須麻神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukubusuma-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} received the Eighth Order of Merit following the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Emi no Oshikatsu Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. After the 11th century, martial Ranks were no longer conferred on deities. ; {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|品位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks|qq=}} : Court Ranks (品位) were primarily reserved for {{ill|imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial family members|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}. Few examples exist of these Ranks being granted to deities. In 749 (Tenpyō Shōhō 1), for example, the {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman God|1=Hachiman}} of {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Shrine|1=Usa Jingū}} was granted the First Class, and the accompanying {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-no-Kami|1=Himegami}} was granted the Second Class. Ranks were distinguished from {{ill|shrine Ranks|ja|社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shakaku|WD=Q10444029}}, which applied to the shrines themselves. Much like human Ranks, deity Ranks initially came with land or stipends but later became symbolic honors. Shrine Ranks ultimately overshadowed deity Ranks in importance. == Historical Developments == === Six National Histories: Deity Ranks === By the conclusion of the {{ill|Rikkokushi|en|Rikkokushi|WD=Q1151168|lt=Six National Histories|1=Rikkokushi}}, deity Ranks had been widely conferred. The following is a summary of higher deity Ranks as recorded by 887. === Same-Time Elevations === From the mid-Heian period onward, deities often received simultaneous Rank elevations as a group. For instance: * {{ill|Kashō 3|ja|嘉祥3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (850): Elevation of all deities as a royal favor during an imperial enthronement. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * "Shinkai" in {{ill|Dictionary of National History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of National History|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}). {{Shinto Horizontal}} ==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] {{Wiktionary|神階|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrines}} {{translated page|ja|神階|version=103856860|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:神階]] [[en:Shinkai (divine rank)]] [[ja:Shinkai (divine rank)]] {{wikidata link|Q11591025}} [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
